figures.caption: Table 4: Stage 1 factual observations about the NPC. The VLM is prompted to answer each with a closed-set value; it never outputs a strategy label. We use Guile (the NPC in SF2) as an example.
Figure 5: Action control in ReactiveGWMbase. The player-controlled character is denoted by a ▲ triangle. The specific action button mappings for each game are detailed in Appendix A.
Table 1: Quantitative comparison of game world models.

Table 3: The 13 discrete behaviors and the physical buttons pressed in each game. Directional keys are shared across the two titles; only the attack buttons differ (Table 2). Behaviors 11 and 12 are forward/back jumps and combine UP with a horizontal key.
Figure 6: Comparison between the vanilla model and ReactiveGWM under the same strategy. The NPC is indicated by the ▲ triangle.

Figure 2: Overview of the data construction and strategy annotation pipeline. Each sample consists of a triplet: a video clip, player actions, and an NPC prompt. Unlike a vanilla prompt that entangles the dynamics between the player and NPC, the NPC prompt provides high-level strategy guidance.
Figure 8: Visualization of the steerable NPC executing distinct strategies in SF2 game. The NPC is denoted by the ▲ triangle.
Figure 11: User Study Part 2 in NPC Strategy Following. Per-class and overall human strategyclassification accuracy.
Figure 10: User Study Part 1 in Player Action Following. Mean participant scores (± SEM) on a 1–5 Likert scale, where higher scores indicate better action following.
Figure 4: Overview of ReactiveGWM training and training-free transfer to the different game.
